figures.caption: Figure 5: Test example of the plate subjected to flat stress. The upper left shows the distribution of the actual elastic modulus (Ground Truth), while the upper right shows the average prediction of that field. The bottom row shows the lower and upper limits of that field, on the left and right, respectively.

Figure 6: Second test example of the plate subjected to plane stress. The upper left shows the distribution of the actual elastic modulus (Ground Truth), while the upper right shows the average prediction of that field. The bottom row shows the lower and upper limits of that field, on the left and right, respectively.
Figure 14: Relative RMSE error for test data for the new (shorter) 3D hyperelastic Beam problem.
Figure 13: Comparative of geometries of trained beam and the new, shorter one.
Figure 4: Relative RMSE error for training and test data for the problem of a 2D plate subjected to plane stress with nonlinear elastic modulus distributions.

Figure 12: Third test example of the hyperelastic beam subjected to bending. The upper left shows the distribution of the applied load (Ground Truth) together with the displacement field that serves as input to the neural network, while the upper right shows the average prediction of the load value and its location. The bottom row shows the lower and upper limits of the load value distributions, on the left and right, respectively.

Figure 11: Second test example of the hyperelastic beam subjected to bending. The upper left shows the distribution of the applied load (Ground Truth) together with the displacement field that serves as input to the neural network, while the upper right shows the average prediction of the load value and its location. The bottom row shows the lower and upper limits of the load value distributions, on the left and right, respectively.
Figure 9: Relative RMSE error for training and test data for the 3D Hyperelastic Beam problem.

Figure 10: Test example of the hyperelastic beam subjected to bending. The upper left shows the distribution of the applied load (ground truth) together with the displacement field that serves as input to the neural network, while the upper right shows the average prediction of the load value and its location. The bottom row shows the lower and upper limits of the load value distributions, on the left and right, respectively.
Figure 2: 2D Plate with a nonlinear distribution of the elastic modulus under plane stress.
Figure 1: A sketch of the Variational Graph Neural Network Architecture.

Figure 17: Test example of the new hyperelastic beam subjected to bending. Figure 8: Mesh (left) and graph (right) associated with the hyperelastic beam problem.

Figure 7: Third example test of the plate subjected to flat stress. The upper left shows the distribution of the actual elastic modulus (Ground Truth), while the upper right shows the average prediction of that field. The bottom row shows the lower and upper limits of that field, on the left and right, respectively.

Figure 3: Three examples of experiments used in neural network training. The first row represents the actual distribution of the Elastic Module, the next two rows represent the corresponding displacements –horizontal and vertical, respectively– for the behavior of the material represented in the initial row.

Figure 15: Test example of the new hyperelastic beam subjected to bending.
